narrative:
Bramblewood Legal is a five-person real-estate practice: a Managing
Partner who signs the engagement letters, two attorneys and a paralegal
who run the matters, and a Records Clerk who keeps the file room and the
books. Matter confidentiality is the house religion; the client folder is
readable by the matter team and the Managing Partner, nobody else, and the
firm's PERMISSIONS.md is treated as seriously as any pleading. Documents
read like a careful small firm wrote them: engagement letters with defined
scopes and signature blocks, kickoff memos that list the parcels and
filings in play, working-session minutes naming every attendee, and status
reports a client's general counsel can forward upward without a call.
Tone is precise and unhurried; dates and dollar figures appear exactly
where the record requires them and nowhere else.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0002",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Zoning Variance Petition for Foley Group

authors.name: Vanessa Brown
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Vanessa Brown runs the firm the way she drafts an engagement letter: scope first, signature last, nothing left implied. She reads every document that leaves the office under the firm's name and will send a draft back over a single loose antecedent or an unearned adjective. Her writing voice is formal but warm at the edges, the tone of someone who has calmed nervous clients across a closing table for most of her career, and she treats access rules with the same gravity as court deadlines.

participants.name: Nicholas Cox
participants.title: Senior Attorney
participants.persona: Nicholas Cox is the firm's steady hand on complicated matters, a title-and-survey obsessive who believes most disputes die quietly when the kickoff memo lists every parcel and filing before anyone gets clever. He writes in short declarative sentences, front-loads the conclusion, and keeps his skepticism for anything a counterparty calls standard language. Colleagues bring him their hardest questions because he answers slowly, once, and correctly.

participants.name: Ashley Reese
participants.title: Attorney
participants.persona: Ashley Reese drafts the status reports clients actually forward upward, and she takes visible pride in that; her memos anticipate the general counsel's next question and answer it a paragraph early. She is the quickest correspondent in the office, courteous and exact, though her internal notes carry a drier wit than anything a client ever sees. She keeps her matter folders in impeccable order and expects the same of anyone she shares them with.

docs.engagement_summary: Zoning Variance Petition for Foley Group, running about 4 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.
